does: This paper proposes ManiF-SMC, a machine unlearning method that operates in representation space rather than through label manipulation or gradient reversal. The core contribution reformulates approximate unlearning as pushing erased samples away from their original learned manifold representation centroids toward their nearest semantic neighbors in retained data. The method leverages self mode connectivity—a geometric property of neural network loss landscapes—to guide this forgetting process while maintaining model performance on retained data. By working purely in representation space, the approach reduces reliance on labels and task-specific gradients, addressing limitations of prior methods that undermine original learning objectives and fail to approximate retraining behavior.
connects: This work builds on the expanding literature in machine unlearning, which aims to enforce privacy rights by removing learned information about specific data points without full retraining. It addresses well-known limitations of earlier approaches like SISA, gradient reversal, and label manipulation methods that either compromise model utility or provide weak unlearning guarantees. The paper connects to research on neural network loss landscapes and mode connectivity, particularly leveraging insights about how retrained models classify forgotten samples based on semantic similarity. It also engages with representation learning theory, recognizing that forgetting can be understood geometrically through manifold structure rather than only through optimization-based approaches.
enables: This work opens several directions for practical privacy-preserving machine learning. By achieving approximate unlearning that better matches retraining behavior, it enables more trustworthy deployment of models subject to right-to-be-forgotten requests without expensive full retraining. The representation-space approach is agnostic to specific tasks and labels, potentially extending unlearning to multi-task, continual learning, and heterogeneous data deletion scenarios. The geometric insights about manifold forgetting could inform new unlearning algorithms with stronger privacy-utility tradeoffs. Future work could explore theoretical guarantees on privacy loss, scalability to large-scale models, and applications to other modalities beyond standard supervised learning classification tasks.
